title: Limiting Particles — だけ / しか / ばかり / でも

meta.focus: Four ways to say 'only': だけ (just/only — neutral), しか…ない (nothing but — always negative), ばかり (nothing but as habit / just completed), and でも (even X / any~).

sections.title: Four Ways to Say 'Only'
sections.summary: Japanese has four particles that express different kinds of 'only'. だけ is neutral and flexible — it simply marks a limit. しか is its grammatical twin: it means the same thing but ALWAYS pairs with a negative verb. ばかり expresses an excessive or exclusive focus (often with a critical tone) and doubles as a 'just completed' marker after a plain past verb. でも means 'even X' after a noun and creates open-ended expressions like 何でも (anything) and いつでも (anytime) when combined with question words.
sections.whyItMatters: These four particles are essential for expressing precision and nuance in everyday Japanese. Without them you can say 'I eat' or 'I drink' — but not 'I only eat this,' 'I just finished eating,' or 'even a child can do this.' Mastering the だけ/しか distinction alone unlocks dozens of natural expressions used every day. ばかり and でも round out the set, letting you describe exclusive habits and inclusive possibilities.
youWillLearn: だけ — only/just (neutral): 水だけ飲みます (I only drink water — my choice)
しか — nothing but (requires negative): 水しか飲みません (I drink nothing but water — always negative!)
だけ vs しか — same meaning, opposite grammar, different nuance
ばかり — nothing but (excessive habit): ゲームばかりしています (all you do is play games)
ばかり — just completed: 食べたばかりです (I just ate)
でも — even X: 子どもでも分かります (even a child understands)
でも — any~ with question words: 何でも (anything), いつでも (anytime)

sections.explanation: だけ follows a noun or dictionary-form verb to express a simple, neutral limit. The verb may be affirmative or negative — unlike しか, there is no grammatical requirement on the verb form. だけ does not carry emotional weight; it simply states that this is the extent of the situation.
notes: だけ can follow nouns (水だけ), verbs in dictionary form (できるだけ), and adjectives.
When you want to mark a deliberate choice or preference, だけ sounds informational and neutral — no frustration implied.
できるだけ ('as much as possible') is a common fixed expression formed from できる (to be able to / possible) + だけ (just/only).

examples.breakdown: 水だけ = only water; だけ follows the noun being limited; the verb is affirmative — neutral limit, no frustration

parts.gloss: as much as possible (fixed: できる + だけ = 'only what is possible')

examples.en: I eat as much as possible.
examples.breakdown: できるだけ is a fixed expression: できる (can do / be possible) + だけ (just/only) = 'only what I can' → 'as much as possible'

sections.meaning: nothing but / only (ALWAYS with negative verb)
sections.explanation: しか expresses an exclusive limit, but it ALWAYS requires a negative verb. The particle and the negative verb work together: しか + ～ない/ません = 'only.' Grammatically, the sentence looks negative — but the meaning is 'nothing but this.' The nuance leans toward insufficiency or restriction.
notes: Critical rule: しか MUST be followed by a negative verb form. Using しか with a positive verb is ungrammatical.
しか carries an emotional nuance of insufficiency: 一人しか来ませんでした = only one person came (and that feels like too few).
Compare: 水だけ飲みます (neutral — I drink only water) vs 水しか飲みません (the restriction is notable — I drink nothing but water).

Important: しか does NOT specify WHY the limit exists. 牛肉しか食べません could mean 'beef is all I can get' (circumstance) OR 'beef is all I ever eat' (personal habit). Context tells you which — the grammar is neutral on this point. Do not assume しか implies external scarcity or forced restriction.

examples.breakdown: 牛肉しか = nothing but beef; しか forces the verb to be negative; 食べません (don't eat) + しか together carry the 'only' meaning

examples.en: I didn't drink anything except coffee.
examples.breakdown: コーヒーしか = nothing but coffee; past negative verb (飲みませんでした) pairs with しか — the restriction applies to the past

sections.title: だけ vs しか — Same Meaning, Opposite Grammar

items.label: だけ + affirmative verb
points: Verb is AFFIRMATIVE (食べます, 飲みます)
Neutral tone — states a limit without emotional weight
The speaker chose or accepts this limit calmly

items.label: しか + negative verb
points: Verb is NEGATIVE (食べません, 飲みません)
The negative verb + しか together = 'only / nothing but'

example.en: I don't eat anything except vegetables. (restricted)

sections.tip: Both sentences describe the same situation — vegetables are the only thing being eaten. But だけ uses an affirmative verb (sounds deliberate and calm), and しか uses a negative verb (sounds restricted or insufficient).

sections.explanation: ばかり has two distinct uses. First: after a noun or dictionary-form verb, it means 'nothing but' — often with a critical or exasperated tone about an excessive habit. This is stronger than neutral だけ. Second: after a verb in plain past form, it means 'just completed' — the action happened very recently.
notes: Habit use: [Noun]ばかり + verb = 'nothing but X.' ゲームばかりしている = doing nothing but playing games (implies criticism).
Just-completed use: [Verb-plain-past]ばかり = 'just did ~.' 食べたばかりです = I just ate.
The 'just completed' use (plain past + ばかり) works like English 'I've just...' or 'I just finished...'

parts.gloss: nothing but games (ゲーム + ばかり — excessive habit)

examples.en: My younger brother does nothing but play games.
examples.breakdown: ゲームばかり = nothing but games; ばかり after a noun has a critical, exasperated tone that neutral だけ does not

parts.gloss: ate (plain past — used before ばかり in the 'just did' pattern)
just (marks recent completion of the action)

examples.breakdown: Plain past verb + ばかり = just completed; 食べた (ate) + ばかりです = I just ate (a moment ago)

sections.explanation: でも after a noun means 'even X' — including the least expected or most minimal case still applies. When combined with question words (何, いつ, どこ, 誰), it creates open-ended 'any~' expressions: 何でも (anything), いつでも (anytime), どこでも (anywhere), 誰でも (anyone). Important: sentence-initial でも (p_demo_but) means 'but/however' and is a completely different usage.
notes: After a noun: [Noun]でも = 'even X.' 子どもでも = even a child; 一人でも = even one person.
With question words: 何でも (anything), いつでも (anytime), どこでも (anywhere), 誰でも (anyone).
Do not confuse with sentence-initial でも (= 'but/however', p_demo_but). でも、行きます = 'But I'll go.' The particle でも (p_demo) comes after a noun; the conjunction でも (p_demo_but) starts a sentence.

examples.breakdown: 子どもでも = even a child; でも after a noun = 'even X' — the least expected case still applies to the statement

parts.gloss: anything / whatever (何 = what/any + でも = any~ marker)

examples.breakdown: Question word + でも = any~: 何でも (anything), いつでも (anytime), どこでも (anywhere), 誰でも (anyone)

examples.note: だけ + affirmative verb = neutral limit. No emotional weight — just a fact about what was eaten today.

parts.gloss: during the trip (旅行 = trip, で = at/during)
nothing but Japanese (日本語 + しか)

examples.en: During the trip I only spoke Japanese.
examples.note: しか + negative verb = exclusive restriction. This implies a deliberate challenge — speaking only Japanese even when another language was available.

examples.context: ばかり — excessive ongoing habit (critical tone)

parts.gloss: nothing but games (ゲーム + ばかり)

examples.en: My younger brother does nothing but play games.
examples.note: ばかり + progressive = excessive ongoing habit. The speaker sounds exasperated — ばかり has a critical undertone that neutral だけ does not carry.

examples.context: でも — open invitation (question word + でも)

parts.gloss: anytime (いつ = when + でも = any~ marker)
come (te-form — connects to ください)

examples.note: いつでも = anytime (question word いつ + でも). With question words, でも creates warm, open-ended expressions.

sections.title: 昼休みの会話 (Lunch Break Conversation)
sections.context: けん and りきぞ are talking during their lunch break.

lines.jp: りきぞさん、今日は昼ご飯を食べましたか。
lines.en: Rikizo, did you eat lunch today?

lines.jp: 今日はパンだけ食べました。
朝はコーヒーしか飲みませんでした。
lines.en: In the morning I only had coffee.

lines.jp: きのうも牛肉ばかり食べていましたね。
lines.en: Yesterday you were eating nothing but beef too, weren't you.

lines.jp: 野菜も食べてください！
分かりました。今日の晩ご飯は何でも食べます！
lines.en: I understand. Tonight I'll eat anything for dinner!

sections.title: Choose the Correct Limiting Particle
sections.instructions: Fill in the blank with the best limiting particle: だけ, しか, ばかり, or でも.

items.explanation: だけ pairs with an AFFIRMATIVE verb (飲みます). しか would require a negative verb (飲みません). ばかり after a noun would imply an excessive habit with a critical tone; でも doesn't work here. だけ = neutral 'only.'

items.translation: I don't eat anything except vegetables.
items.explanation: しか ALWAYS pairs with a NEGATIVE verb (食べません). The negative verb form is the key signal that しか is needed. だけ would require an affirmative verb (食べます). ばかり and でも don't fit the negative-verb structure here.

ばかり after a noun + progressive verb expresses an excessive, ongoing habit — often with an exasperated or critical tone. ゲームばかりしている = doing nothing but playing games. だけ would also work grammatically but lacks the critical nuance ばかり adds.
でも after a noun means 'even X.' 子どもでも = even a child. This is p_demo (inclusive particle) — distinct from sentence-initial でも (p_demo_but) which means 'but/however.'
Plain past verb + ばかり = 'just completed the action.' 食べた (plain past of 食べる) + ばかりです = 'I just ate (a moment ago).' This is ばかり's second use — quite different from the noun + ばかり habit use.

sections.title: Limiting Particle Practice
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: Which sentence correctly uses しか?
items.explanation: しか ALWAYS requires a NEGATIVE verb. 水しか飲みます (positive verb) is ungrammatical. 水しか飲みません (negative verb) is correct: 'I drink nothing but water.' The negative verb and しか work together to create the 'only' meaning.

items.q: What does 食べたばかりです mean?
items.explanation: Verb (plain past) + ばかりです = 'just completed the action.' 食べた (ate — plain past) + ばかり = just ate. This is ばかり's 'just completed' use, different from the habit use (noun + ばかり = nothing but).

items.q: How do you say 'I eat anything' in Japanese?
choices: 何ばかり食べています。
items.explanation: Question word + でも = any~: 何でも = anything, いつでも = anytime, どこでも = anywhere. 何でも食べます = 'I eat anything / whatever.' The でも here is the inclusive particle (p_demo), not the 'but' conjunction.

items.q: 牛肉だけ食べます vs 牛肉しか食べません — what is the key grammatical difference?
choices: だけ uses an affirmative verb; しか uses a negative verb.
だけ means 'even beef'; しか means 'only beef'.
They use the same verb form; only the emotional tone differs.
だけ is used for food; しか is used for drinks.
items.answer: だけ uses an affirmative verb; しか uses a negative verb.
items.explanation: Both sentences mean roughly 'I only eat beef' but use opposite verb forms. だけ + affirmative verb (食べます) is neutral and deliberate. しか + negative verb (食べません) implies restriction or insufficiency. The verb polarity is the key grammatical difference.

items.q: 一人しか来ませんでした。 What does this sentence mean?
items.explanation: 一人 (one person) + しか + 来ませんでした (did not come — polite past negative). しか + negative verb = 'only/nothing but': only one person came (and the speaker implies that was not many enough).
